moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and controlling the important e5 and c5 squares. This move is the foundation of many classical opening systems and gives us excellent flexibility in our development. The d4 pawn supports potential advances in the center and allows our pieces to develop naturally, particularly our dark-squared bishop which can later go to f4 or g5. By controlling central squares from the start, we're following the classical principle of contesting central control before developing pieces.

Black develops the knight to its ideal square while immediately putting pressure on our central pawn. This move is incredibly flexible and doesn't commit Black to any particular pawn structure yet - they're keeping their options open for both King's Indian setups with g6 and Bg7, or Nimzo-Indian ideas with Bb4+. The knight on f6 also prepares to support central counterplay with moves like e6 or g6 followed by d5. We should note that Black is following hypermodern principles, allowing us central space while preparing to challenge it later.

We're playing the English Opening move order, supporting our d4 pawn while gaining additional control over the central d5 and b5 squares. This move creates the foundation for what will likely become a Queen's Gambit or English Opening structure, and it prevents Black from easily establishing a pawn on d5. The c4 pawn also supports potential expansion with b4-b5 on the queenside and gives our pieces more scope. This is a critical move in the English/Neo-Grünfeld systems, as it allows us to transpose into various favorable setups depending on how Black responds.

Black is committing to a King's Indian Defense or Grünfeld Defense setup, preparing to fianchetto the dark-squared bishop on g7. This move signals Black's intention to fight for the center from a hypermodern perspective, allowing us space while preparing counterplay against our center. The g6 move also prepares to castle kingside safely and creates potential tactical motifs on the long diagonal. Black is essentially saying 'I'll let you have the center for now, but I'm going to put maximum pressure on it once my pieces are developed.' We should be prepared for sharp tactical play and central tension.

We're adopting a solid, positional approach by preparing to fianchetto our own dark-squared bishop on g2, creating what's known as the Neo-Grünfeld or English Opening setup. This move allows us to control the long diagonal and put pressure on Black's center, particularly the d5 square which Black will likely want to occupy. Our bishop on g2 will be a powerful piece, supporting both central control and potential kingside attacks. By playing g3, we're indicating our intention to play a more positional game rather than sharp tactical lines, though this system can certainly lead to complex middlegame battles.

Black strikes immediately at the heart of our pawn center, creating the characteristic Grünfeld pawn structure where Black challenges our space advantage head-on. This move is the defining characteristic of the Grünfeld Defense - Black is willing to allow us a strong pawn center in exchange for active piece play and counterattacking chances. The d5 thrust puts immediate pressure on our c4 pawn and opens lines for Black's pieces, particularly the light-squared bishop which can now develop actively. We now face a critical decision about how to handle the central tension - we can capture with cxd5, support with e3, or even ignore it temporarily while developing our pieces.
